question_text:
Odd Even Jump

You are given an integer array arr. From some starting index, you can make a series of jumps. The (1st, 3rd, 5th, ...) jumps in the series are called odd-numbered jumps, and the (2nd, 4th, 6th, ...) jumps in the series are called even-numbered jumps. Note that the jumps are numbered, not the indices.

You may jump forward from index i to index j (with i < j) in the following way:

  • During odd-numbered jumps (i.e., jumps 1, 3, 5, ...), you jump to the index j such that arr[i] <= arr[j] and arr[j] is the smallest possible value. If there are multiple such indices j, you can only jump to the smallest such index j.
  • During even-numbered jumps (i.e., jumps 2, 4, 6, ...), you jump to the index j such that arr[i] >= arr[j] and arr[j] is the largest possible value. If there are multiple such indices j, you can only jump to the smallest such index j.

It may be the case that for some index i, there are no legal jumps.

A starting index is good if, starting from that index, you can reach the end of the array (index arr.length - 1) by jumping some number of times (possibly 0 or more than once).

Return the number of good starting indices.
input_format: A single line containing space-separated integers representing the array `arr`.
output_format: A single integer denoting the number of good starting indices.
explanation:
This is a dynamic programming problem heavily relying on efficient next-jump identification via a monotonic stack approach after sorting.

First, we need to know for each index `i`, what is the destination of an odd jump (`next_higher`) and an even jump (`next_lower`). We can achieve this by pairing the elements with their indices, sorting them, and using a monotonic decreasing stack to find the next greater or equal element's index. For identical elements, sorting orders them by index to inherently satisfy the 'smallest index' tie-breaking rule.

After identifying `next_higher` and `next_lower` arrays, we define two DP boolean arrays: `higher[i]` (true if we can reach the end starting with an odd jump from `i`) and `lower[i]` (true if we can reach the end starting with an even jump from `i`). 
Processing from right to left, we use state transitions `higher[i] = lower[next_higher[i]]` and `lower[i] = higher[next_lower[i]]`. Initial setup implies `higher[N-1] = lower[N-1] = True`.

The overall answer is the number of indices where `higher[i]` is True, yielding an optimal O(N log N) runtime.
